sections.title: Why Complexity Analysis matters
sections.content:
Complexity analysis is the language interviewers use to compare two correct solutions and choose the one that scales.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Big-O trade-offs, practical estimation, and explaining complexity clearly.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 19, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Complexity Analysis
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Complexity analysis is the language interviewers use to compare two correct solutions and choose the one that scales.

### Core interview mental model
For Complexity Analysis, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Complexity Analysis are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Quoting Big-O without explaining dominant operation.
Ignoring space complexity while optimizing time.
Confusing worst-case and average-case in answers.
Using nested loops without analyzing actual iteration bounds.
Giving complexity from memory instead of deriving it step-by-step.

sections.title: Exercise - Complexity Analysis Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Complexity Analysis**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What does Big-O measure?
conceptual.answer: Upper bound on how runtime or memory grows as input size n increases—ignore constants and lower-order terms.

conceptual.question: Difference between O(n) and O(n²)?
conceptual.answer: Linear work scales proportionally to n; quadratic doubles impact when n doubles—nested loops over data often yield O(n²).

conceptual.question: What is amortized analysis?
conceptual.answer: Average cost per operation over a sequence—e.g., dynamic array append is O(1) amortized though occasional resize costs O(n).

conceptual.question: What is Ω and Θ?
conceptual.answer: Ω is lower bound; Θ means tight bound (same growth upper and lower)—interviews often focus on Big-O upper bounds.

conceptual.question: Why compare algorithms asymptotically?
conceptual.answer: Predict behavior at large n; constants matter for small n but scalability dominates in system design.

conceptual.question: What is space complexity?
conceptual.answer: Extra memory beyond input—O(1) extra means constant auxiliary space regardless of n.

conceptual.question: What is worst-case vs average-case?
conceptual.answer: Worst guarantees under adversarial input; average assumes distribution—both matter for different SLAs.

conceptual.question: How do you analyze a single loop over array?
conceptual.answer: Typically O(n) if body is O(1) per element.

conceptual.question: How do you analyze nested loops?
conceptual.answer: Multiply depths if each depends on n—e.g., i and j both to n often O(n²).

conceptual.question: What is binary search complexity?
conceptual.answer: O(log n) on sorted array—halves search space each step.

conceptual.question: What is hash map average lookup?
conceptual.answer: O(1) average for get/put; O(n) worst case if many collisions—know your hash function and load factor.

conceptual.question: What is the master theorem (sketch)?
conceptual.answer: Recurrence T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n) solves many divide-and-conquer recurrences—compare f(n) to n^(log_b a).

conceptual.question: Why can sorting dominate complexity?
conceptual.answer: O(n log n) sort + O(n) scan may beat O(n²) brute force for structured problems.

conceptual.question: What is pseudo-polynomial?
conceptual.answer: Looks polynomial in numeric value but exponential in bit length—relevant for subset-sum style DP.

conceptual.question: How does parallelism change Big-O?
conceptual.answer: Big-O is sequential steps; wall-clock may drop with parallel hardware but work complexity may stay the same.

codeBased.question: Given nested for i,j to n, state complexity.
codeBased.answer: O(n²) time, O(1) extra space if no large aux structures.

codeBased.question: Binary search: why mid = lo + (hi-lo)/2?
codeBased.answer: Avoids integer overflow from (lo+hi)/2 in large indices.

codeBased.question: Count operations in: for i 0..n: for j i..n
codeBased.answer: Roughly n²/2 iterations—still O(n²).

codeBased.question: When does HashMap degrade to O(n)?
codeBased.answer: Many collisions under poor hash or adversarial keys—use balanced structures or crypto hash if needed.

codeBased.question: Space of merge sort vs heap sort?
codeBased.answer: Merge sort O(n) extra; heap sort O(1) extra—trade memory for stability and constants.

codeBased.question: Two-pointer on sorted array for pair sum—complexity?
codeBased.answer: O(n) time after sort O(n log n) if not already sorted.

codeBased.question: Analyze recursion depth for factorial n.
codeBased.answer: O(n) stack frames—may overflow for large n.

codeBased.question: DP with n states and O(1) transition each?
codeBased.answer: O(n) time and O(n) space—or rolling array for O(1) space if applicable.

codeBased.question: Compare BFS vs DFS on graph with V,E.
codeBased.answer: BFS/DFS O(V+E) time with adjacency list.

codeBased.question: What complexity to build heap from n elements?
codeBased.answer: O(n) heapify all elements—not O(n log n) repeated insert for building from array.

seniorScenario.question: Service latency p99 spikes when data doubled—how investigate?
seniorScenario.answer: Profile hot paths, check algorithmic regression (O(n²) creep), DB indexes, and GC—not just CPU%.

seniorScenario.question: Interview: “Is this O(n) or O(n log n)?”—ambiguous code.
seniorScenario.answer: Walk through loops/recursion, state transitions; mention amortized if relevant.

seniorScenario.question: Capacity planning: algorithm O(n²) in batch job—ops fix?
seniorScenario.answer: Shard input, parallelize independent chunks, or replace algorithm; don’t only scale hardware.

seniorScenario.question: Cache complexity in real systems?
seniorScenario.answer: Memory hierarchy effects dominate at scale—locality matters even if Big-O same.

seniorScenario.question: Competitive online algorithms—relate?
seniorScenario.answer: Different model (future unknown); mention when streaming constraints apply.

seniorScenario.question: Team ships “fast” hash map abuse—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Worst-case DoS or unbounded memory—need bounds and monitoring.

wrongAnswers: Big-O tells you exact milliseconds on a given machine.
O(2n) is fundamentally larger than O(n) in asymptotic terms.
Nested loops always mean O(n³).
Space complexity includes the input array itself.
Binary search works on any array without precondition.
HashMap worst-case lookup is always O(1).

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Moving both pointers incorrectly and skipping valid candidates.
Forgetting to update window state when left pointer shifts.
Not defining the window invariant before coding.
Applying two pointers on unsorted data without justification.
Missing edge cases for empty/single-element arrays.

sections.title: Exercise - Arrays and Two Pointers Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Arrays and Two Pointers**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
conceptual.answer: Two indices move through array/string—often from both ends or both forward—to reduce brute force O(n²) to O(n).

conceptual.question: When is two pointers applicable?
conceptual.answer: Sorted arrays, palindromes, pair-sum with ordering, merging sorted lists—monotonic movement exploits structure.

conceptual.question: Difference from sliding window?
conceptual.answer: Sliding window maintains a subarray range; two pointers often meet or chase on fixed structure—related but different patterns.

conceptual.question: What is Dutch national flag problem?
conceptual.answer: Partition array into regions by value with linear scan—related pointer discipline.

conceptual.question: Why sort before two-sum sometimes?
conceptual.answer: Sorting enables moving pointers based on comparison to target—trade index loss if original indices needed.

conceptual.question: What is “collision” of pointers?
conceptual.answer: When left and right pointers cross or meet—termination condition.

conceptual.question: Can two pointers work on linked lists?
conceptual.answer: Yes—detect cycle (Floyd), merge sorted lists, middle node.

conceptual.question: What is three-sum reduction?
conceptual.answer: Fix one element, two-pointer on remainder—O(n²) after sort.

conceptual.question: In-place removal of duplicates sorted array?
conceptual.answer: Slow/fast pointer writes unique elements forward.

conceptual.question: What is trapping rain water intuition?
conceptual.answer: Left/right max arrays or two pointers with running max—O(n) time.

conceptual.question: Two-pointer vs binary search?
conceptual.answer: Binary search halves search space on sorted data; two pointers often scan with two moving indices simultaneously.

conceptual.question: What if input not sorted for pair sum?
conceptual.answer: Hash map O(n) space for complements, or sort losing stability of indices—choose per requirements.

conceptual.question: Stability of partition algorithms?
conceptual.answer: Some partition schemes stable, many quicksort partitions are not—know interview variant.

conceptual.question: What is merge step in merge sort?
conceptual.answer: Two pointers walk two sorted halves—classic pattern.

conceptual.question: Edge case: empty or length-1 array?
conceptual.answer: Pointers may not enter loop—always test boundaries.

codeBased.question: Palindrome check with two pointers on char array.
codeBased.answer: while (l<r) if (s[l++]!=s[r--]) return false; return true;

codeBased.question: Pair sum sorted: move lo/hi based on sum vs target.
codeBased.answer: if sum<target lo++; else if sum>target hi--; else found.

codeBased.question: Remove duplicates in-place sorted array return new length.
codeBased.answer: Slow pointer tracks write position; fast scans.

codeBased.question: Merge two sorted arrays without extra space (if fits).
codeBased.answer: Fill from end when merging into buffer with space at end.

codeBased.question: Container with most water: move shorter line.
codeBased.answer: Greedy two-pointer proof often asked—know intuition.

codeBased.question: Three-sum avoid duplicates after sort.
codeBased.answer: Skip equal adjacent values when advancing pointers.

codeBased.question: Fast/slow pointer start same for cycle detection.
codeBased.answer: If fast meets slow, cycle exists.

codeBased.question: Partition array around pivot (Lomuto/Hoare sketch).
codeBased.answer: Know one variant’s loop invariants.

codeBased.question: Two arrays merge pointer i,j.
codeBased.answer: Compare a[i], b[j], advance smaller—like merge step.

codeBased.question: Minimum window substring—related?
codeBased.answer: Sliding window with need counts—often separate pattern but contrasts with simple two-pointer.

seniorScenario.question: Image processing pipeline O(n²) per frame—missed SLA.
seniorScenario.answer: Downsample, GPU offload, or algorithm change; profile before blaming GC.

seniorScenario.question: Security: two-pointer on user string in C—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Buffer bounds—Java safer but still validate lengths for DoS.

seniorScenario.question: Streaming data: can’t sort full array?
seniorScenario.answer: Use heaps, reservoir sampling, or approximate algorithms—two-pointer may not apply.

seniorScenario.question: Parallel two-pointer patterns?
seniorScenario.answer: Harder—partition first then parallel merge; sequential two-pointer often inherently sequential.

seniorScenario.question: Database index scan vs two-pointer in app?
seniorScenario.answer: Push work to DB when possible—less data movement.

seniorScenario.question: Regression: “optimized” pair sum wrong on duplicates.
seniorScenario.answer: Add test cases with repeated values and negative numbers.

wrongAnswers: Two pointers always require sorted input.
Two-pointer pair sum is O(n²) after sorting.
Fast/slow pointers only work on arrays, never linked lists.
You never need hash map if you use two pointers.
Three-sum is always O(n log n).
Two pointers from both ends always move both every step.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Losing node references while reversing or deleting.
Forgetting null checks before accessing next pointers.
Mixing node identity and node value comparisons.
Incorrectly handling head/tail updates in edge cases.
Not drawing pointer state before coding complex operations.

sections.title: Exercise - Linked Lists Problem Drill
sections.problem:
Solve one medium-level Java problem for **Linked Lists**.

Requirements:
1. Provide brute-force and optimized approach.
2. Explain why optimized approach is better.
3. Include one edge-case test.
4. Print expected output for sample input.
5. State time/space complexity clearly.
hints: Dry-run on paper for one non-trivial case.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a singly linked list node?
conceptual.answer: Object with value and reference to next—no random access by index.

conceptual.question: Why O(n) to access element i?
conceptual.answer: Must walk from head i steps—unlike array O(1).

conceptual.question: What is dummy head sentinel?
conceptual.answer: Fake node before head simplifies insertion/deletion at head without special cases.

conceptual.question: How detect cycle?
conceptual.answer: Floyd’s tortoise/hare—fast moves 2 steps, slow 1; meeting implies cycle.

conceptual.question: How reverse linked list iteratively?
conceptual.answer: prev/cur/next pointers rewiring—O(n) time, O(1) space.

conceptual.question: Recursive reverse—complexity?
conceptual.answer: O(n) time, O(n) stack space—risk overflow on long lists.

conceptual.question: Merge two sorted lists?
conceptual.answer: Compare heads, attach smaller, advance—like merge sort merge.

conceptual.question: Find middle element?
conceptual.answer: Slow/fast—when fast reaches end, slow at middle (careful even count).

conceptual.question: What is doubly linked list benefit?
conceptual.answer: O(1) delete given node with prev pointer—used in LRU caches.

conceptual.question: Intersection of two lists?
conceptual.answer: Align lengths then walk together—or use hash set of nodes identity.

conceptual.question: Clone graph/list with random pointer?
conceptual.answer: Map old→new nodes—two-pass linking.

conceptual.question: Why avoid stack overflow on deep recursion?
conceptual.answer: Prefer iterative for production-sized lists.

conceptual.question: What is null-terminated list end?
conceptual.answer: next==null marks tail—always check NPE.

conceptual.question: Compare ArrayList vs LinkedList in Java?
conceptual.answer: ArrayList cache-friendly; LinkedList node overhead—most apps prefer ArrayList unless proven need.

conceptual.question: What is skip list (mention)?
conceptual.answer: Probabilistic structure for O(log n) search—alternative to balanced trees.

codeBased.question: Write iterative reverse of singly linked list.
codeBased.answer: Classic three-pointer while loop.

codeBased.question: Detect cycle return node or boolean?
codeBased.answer: Know Floyd variant to find start of cycle.

codeBased.question: Merge two sorted linked lists recursively.
codeBased.answer: Compare head1.data vs head2.data, recurse.

codeBased.question: Remove nth node from end one pass.
codeBased.answer: Two pointers n apart or length minus n.

codeBased.question: Add two numbers represented as reversed digit lists.
codeBased.answer: Carry digit, create new list.

codeBased.question: Palindrome linked list O(1) space?
codeBased.answer: Reverse second half, compare halves.

codeBased.question: Delete node with only pointer to that node (not head).
codeBased.answer: Copy next value into node and delete next—classic trick.

codeBased.question: Deep copy list with random pointer—sketch.
codeBased.answer: HashMap original→copy; second pass assign random.

codeBased.question: Rotate list by k places.
codeBased.answer: Connect tail to head, find new break point.

codeBased.question: Middle of linked list—even length pick second?
codeBased.answer: Clarify in interview which middle definition.

seniorScenario.question: Concurrent modification of LinkedList—fail-fast?
seniorScenario.answer: Iterator throws CME; use concurrent structures or synchronize.

seniorScenario.question: Memory overhead of LinkedList in JVM?
seniorScenario.answer: Node objects + references—GC pressure vs ArrayList for large sequences.

seniorScenario.question: LRU cache implementation choice?
seniorScenario.answer: HashMap + doubly linked list for O(1) get/put eviction.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed linked structures?
seniorScenario.answer: Usually not—use IDs and DB relations; pointer swizzling in ORMs.

seniorScenario.question: Stack overflow in recursive list walk on huge list?
seniorScenario.answer: Switch to iterative or increase stack (last resort).

seniorScenario.question: Serialization of cyclic list?
seniorScenario.answer: Need protocol—by design acyclic or break cycle with sentinel IDs.

wrongAnswers: Linked list supports O(1) random access by index.
Floyd cycle detection always finds cycle length in first pass.
Java LinkedList is always faster than ArrayList for all workloads.
Reversing a list requires O(n) extra space always.
Dummy head changes asymptotic complexity.
Merge sorted lists cannot be done recursively.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Using Stack class blindly instead of modern Deque.
Forgetting to pop stale elements in monotonic structures.
Confusing stack top with queue front/back semantics.
Missing empty-structure guards before peek/pop.
Not proving amortized complexity for repeated push/pop patterns.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a stack (LIFO)?
conceptual.answer: Push/pop at one end—call stacks, undo, DFS.

conceptual.question: What is a queue (FIFO)?
conceptual.answer: Enqueue tail, dequeue head—BFS, scheduling.

conceptual.question: Java Deque implementations?
conceptual.answer: ArrayDeque (resizable array) often preferred over LinkedList for stacks/queues.

conceptual.question: What is priority queue?
conceptual.answer: Heap-backed—poll returns highest (or lowest) priority element.

conceptual.question: Stack with min in O(1)?
conceptual.answer: Auxiliary stack tracking min so far or store pairs (value, currentMin).

conceptual.question: Monotonic stack use?
conceptual.answer: Next greater element, histogram problems—maintain increasing/decreasing property.

conceptual.question: BFS with queue on graph?
conceptual.answer: Level-order tree traversal; shortest path in unweighted graph.

conceptual.question: Circular buffer / ring queue?
conceptual.answer: Fixed-size array with head/tail pointers—bounded queues.

conceptual.question: BlockingQueue in Java?
conceptual.answer: put/take block for producer-consumer—ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue.

conceptual.question: Stack from queues (interview puzzle)?
conceptual.answer: Possible with two queues—simulate recursion depth.

conceptual.question: What is double-ended queue Deque?
conceptual.answer: addFirst/addLast—used in sliding window max.

conceptual.question: Compare Stack class vs Deque?
conceptual.answer: Stack legacy; prefer Deque interface—Stack extends Vector oddly.

conceptual.question: Amortized cost ArrayDeque push?
conceptual.answer: O(1) amortized resizing.

conceptual.question: What is work-stealing deque?
conceptual.answer: ForkJoinPool parallel tasks—advanced concurrency structure.

conceptual.question: Priority queue with custom comparator?
conceptual.answer: Min vs max heap depending on comparator order.

codeBased.question: Valid parentheses with stack.
codeBased.answer: Push opens, pop match closes; stack empty at end.

codeBased.question: Evaluate RPN with stack.
codeBased.answer: Push numbers, apply operator to two pops.

codeBased.question: BFS level-order binary tree using Queue.
codeBased.answer: Queue of nodes; poll, offer children.

codeBased.question: Sliding window maximum with deque.
codeBased.answer: Remove smaller indices from back when new larger arrives; pop front if out of window.

codeBased.question: Min stack push/pop/getMin all O(1).
codeBased.answer: Auxiliary stack or store (val, min) pairs.

codeBased.question: Implement queue using two stacks.
codeBased.answer: Transfer between stacks amortized O(1).

codeBased.question: Next greater element array—monotonic stack.
codeBased.answer: Iterate, pop while smaller than current.

codeBased.question: DFS iterative using stack.
codeBased.answer: Push nodes, pop explore—mimic recursion.

codeBased.question: PriorityQueue top K elements.
codeBased.answer: Maintain size-k max-heap for smallest K or min-heap for largest K—clarify.

codeBased.question: ArrayDeque as stack—methods?
codeBased.answer: push/pop/peek vs addLast/removeLast/peekLast—be consistent.

seniorScenario.question: Unbounded queue in messaging—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Memory exhaustion; need back-pressure, bounded queues, drop policies.

seniorScenario.question: Priority inversion with priority queue threads?
seniorScenario.answer: OS scheduling issue—mention when discussing real-time systems.

seniorScenario.question: Kafka partition as queue—ordering?
seniorScenario.answer: Per-partition order; parallel consumers across partitions.

seniorScenario.question: Web server request queue overload?
seniorScenario.answer: 503, load shedding, circuit breakers—queue depth metrics.

seniorScenario.question: Thread pool queue full—policy?
seniorScenario.answer: CallerRuns, Abort, Discard—each has trade-offs.

seniorScenario.question: Stack trace depth in production errors?
seniorScenario.answer: Deep recursion or framework bug—monitor stack size.

wrongAnswers: Queue always means FIFO in every Java class named Queue.
PriorityQueue guarantees FIFO among equal priorities.
java.util.Stack is the preferred stack implementation today.
ArrayDeque cannot be used as a LIFO stack.
BFS always uses a stack data structure.
BlockingQueue never blocks the caller.

sections.title: Why HashMaps and Hashing matters
sections.content:
Hashing is the go-to interview tool for fast lookup; the key skill is choosing the right hash key representation.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Collision intuition, key design, frequency maps, and lookup strategy.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 23, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - HashMaps and Hashing
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Hashing is the go-to interview tool for fast lookup; the key skill is choosing the right hash key representation.

### Core interview mental model
For HashMaps and Hashing, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in HashMaps and Hashing are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: How does HashMap work?
conceptual.answer: Buckets from hash(key)%n; collision chains or trees; equals verifies key match.

conceptual.question: equals/hashCode contract?
conceptual.answer: Equal objects must have same hashCode; unequal may collide—must handle collisions.

conceptual.question: Load factor and resize?
conceptual.answer: When entries exceed capacity*loadFactor, table doubles (roughly)—rehash all entries.

conceptual.question: Java 8+ treeify?
conceptual.answer: Long chains convert to balanced trees—O(log n) worst per bucket vs O(n).

conceptual.question: Why immutable keys preferred?
conceptual.answer: If key mutates after insert, bucket lookup breaks.

conceptual.question: What is consistent hashing?
conceptual.answer: Distributed caches—minimize key movement when nodes add/remove.

conceptual.question: Open addressing vs chaining?
conceptual.answer: Alternative collision strategies—Java HashMap uses chaining (with treeify).

conceptual.question: Complexity average vs worst?
conceptual.answer: Average O(1); worst O(n) all collide—design hashes well.

conceptual.question: What is IdentityHashMap?
conceptual.answer: Uses == instead of equals—reference identity keys.

conceptual.question: WeakHashMap use?
conceptual.answer: Entries garbage-collected when key only weakly reachable—caches with auto eviction.

conceptual.question: LinkedHashMap access order?
conceptual.answer: LRU mode moves entries on get—useful for caches.

conceptual.question: ConcurrentHashMap vs Collections.synchronizedMap?
conceptual.answer: CHM finer locking/CAS segments—better concurrent throughput.

conceptual.question: What makes a good hash function?
conceptual.answer: Uniform distribution, fast, uses all key fields relevant to equality.

conceptual.question: Hash flooding attack?
conceptual.answer: Adversarial keys same bucket—mitigate with salted hashing or treeify.

conceptual.question: ComputeIfAbsent?
conceptual.answer: Atomic “get or compute” for maps—careful with recursive compute.

codeBased.question: Count frequencies with HashMap merge.
codeBased.answer: map.merge(k,1,Integer::sum);

codeBased.question: Group list of strings by first char.
codeBased.answer: Map<Character, List<String>> or computeIfAbsent.

codeBased.question: Detect anagrams via character counts hash.
codeBased.answer: Normalize sorted string or count array as key.

codeBased.question: Two-sum complement with HashSet/Map.
codeBased.answer: Store seen values; check target-current in map.

codeBased.question: Implement equals/hashCode for value class.
codeBased.answer: Use Objects.hash(field1,field2); equals on same fields.

codeBased.question: Rehashing on resize—what happens?
codeBased.answer: Buckets redistributed; iterator may fail-fast if concurrent mod.

codeBased.question: Use IdentityHashMap example.
codeBased.answer: Dedup object graph by reference not equals.

codeBased.question: ConcurrentHashMap compute—thread safety?
codeBased.answer: Atomic per-bin; still document compound operations.

codeBased.question: HashSet backed by HashMap—add complexity?
codeBased.answer: Same as map put—O(1) average.

codeBased.question: Bad hash: only high bits—symptom?
codeBased.answer: Collisions cluster in low buckets—mask with spread functions (like HashMap mix).

seniorScenario.question: Redis cluster resharding—conceptual tie?
seniorScenario.answer: Consistent hashing, slot migration—similar problem domain.

seniorScenario.question: Large HashMap pause on resize?
seniorScenario.answer: Long pause if huge table—consider pre-sizing or off-heap structures.

seniorScenario.question: Custom key type in distributed map—serialization?
seniorScenario.answer: Stable equals across versions—schema evolution.

seniorScenario.question: Security: hashDoS on servlet parameter map?
seniorScenario.answer: JDK mitigations; limit input sizes; randomized hash seeds historically.

seniorScenario.question: ORM L2 cache keying—pitfall?
seniorScenario.answer: Include all relevant fields; mutable entities break cache.

seniorScenario.question: Metrics: HashMap memory per entry?
seniorScenario.answer: Footprint of nodes, pointers—tune initial capacity if known size.

wrongAnswers: hashCode must be unique for every distinct object.
Equal objects can have different hashCodes if equals is overridden.
HashMap iteration order matches insertion order by default.
You can safely use mutable StringBuilder as HashMap key.
ConcurrentHashMap allows null keys like HashMap.
Worst-case HashMap get is always O(1).

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: Binary tree vs binary search tree?
conceptual.answer: BST property: left < node < right for all nodes—enables ordered search.

conceptual.question: Tree traversals?
conceptual.answer: Preorder, inorder, postorder, level-order—each serves different use cases.

conceptual.question: BST search complexity?
conceptual.answer: O(h) height; balanced O(log n), skewed O(n).

conceptual.question: What is balanced tree?
conceptual.answer: AVL, Red-Black—guarantee O(log n) height with rotations.

conceptual.question: What is heap?
conceptual.answer: Complete binary tree with heap property—used in priority queues.

conceptual.question: Trie (prefix tree)?
conceptual.answer: Strings share prefixes—efficient autocomplete, O(m) per query length.

conceptual.question: Lowest common ancestor in BST?
conceptual.answer: Walk from root comparing keys—O(h).

conceptual.question: Serialize binary tree?
conceptual.answer: Preorder with null markers or level-order with BFS—reconstruct carefully.

conceptual.question: Diameter of tree?
conceptual.answer: Longest path between any two nodes—often two DFS or height recursion.

conceptual.question: What is segment tree / Fenwick?
conceptual.answer: Range query structures—advanced competitive programming topics.

conceptual.question: Morris traversal?
conceptual.answer: O(1) space inorder—threaded tree trick—rare in interviews but impressive.

conceptual.question: N-ary tree representation?
conceptual.answer: List of children per node—serialize to bracket strings.

conceptual.question: Red-black vs AVL?
conceptual.answer: AVL stricter balance faster lookups more rotations; RB fewer rotations better insert—Java TreeMap is RB.

conceptual.question: B-tree use case?
conceptual.answer: Databases disk blocks—wide nodes reduce height for disk I/O.

conceptual.question: Tree DP on rooted tree?
conceptual.answer: Post-order accumulate subtrees—common pattern.

codeBased.question: Max depth of binary tree recursive.
codeBased.answer: 1+max(left,right) base null 0.

codeBased.question: Validate BST—pass min/max bounds.
codeBased.answer: Or inorder must be strictly increasing.

codeBased.question: Level-order traversal with queue.
codeBased.answer: Queue nodes; track size per level if needed.

codeBased.question: Invert binary tree.
codeBased.answer: Swap children recursively or iteratively.

codeBased.question: Path sum equals target—backtrack or recursion.
codeBased.answer: Track current sum along path.

codeBased.question: Kth smallest in BST?
codeBased.answer: Inorder counter or augmented tree with subtree sizes.

codeBased.question: Lowest common ancestor binary tree (not BST).
codeBased.answer: Post-order return pointer if found in subtrees.

codeBased.question: Same tree structure and values?
codeBased.answer: Simultaneous recursion p,q.

codeBased.question: Serialize to string—example approach.
codeBased.answer: Preorder with "null" for missing.

codeBased.question: Count nodes complete tree—formula?
codeBased.answer: If complete, use math; else recurse—interview variant.

seniorScenario.question: DB index B-tree vs app in-memory tree?
seniorScenario.answer: Different cost models—disk page size matters.

seniorScenario.question: GC and deep object graphs of trees?
seniorScenario.answer: Stack depth for traversal; prefer iterative if millions of nodes.

seniorScenario.question: XML/JSON DOM trees memory?
seniorScenario.answer: SAX vs DOM—streaming vs tree—large files.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed consensus tree (Raft log)—related?
seniorScenario.answer: Append-only log structure—not binary tree but ordered thinking.

seniorScenario.question: Decision tree ML model serialization?
seniorScenario.answer: Different domain—avoid confusing with CS trees in same answer.

seniorScenario.question: Threaded binary tree in UI component trees?
seniorScenario.answer: React fiber-like structures—traversal for rendering.

wrongAnswers: Inorder traversal of any binary tree always yields sorted order.
BST height is always log n.
Heaps allow efficient search for arbitrary element in O(1).
Binary tree must have exactly two children per node.
TreeMap is implemented as a simple linked list.
Trie can only store English lowercase letters.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a binary heap?
conceptual.answer: Complete binary tree with heap property: min-heap parent ≤ children; max-heap opposite.

conceptual.question: Array representation of heap?
conceptual.answer: Index i: children 2i+1,2i+2; parent (i-1)/2—no pointers needed.

conceptual.question: Heapify up vs down?
conceptual.answer: After insert sift up; after replace root sift down—O(log n).

conceptual.question: Build heap from array complexity?
conceptual.answer: O(n) bottom-up heapify—not O(n log n) n inserts.

conceptual.question: PriorityQueue in Java min or max?
conceptual.answer: Min-heap by default; reverse Comparator for max.

conceptual.question: What is d-ary heap?
conceptual.answer: More children per node—shallower, wider—used in some algorithms.

conceptual.question: Heap sort steps?
conceptual.answer: Build max heap, swap root with end, heapify reduced heap—O(n log n) in-place unstable.

conceptual.question: Median from two heaps?
conceptual.answer: Low max-heap + high min-heap balance—O(log n) insert, O(1) median.

conceptual.question: Fibonacci heap (mention)?
conceptual.answer: Theoretical improvements for decrease-key—rare in practice interviews.

conceptual.question: When heap beats sorting full array?
conceptual.answer: When only top K needed—not full sort.

conceptual.question: Indexed priority queue?
conceptual.answer: Supports decrease-key by index—Dijkstra optimizations.

conceptual.question: Latency of poll vs peek?
conceptual.answer: peek O(1) root; poll O(log n) restore heap.

conceptual.question: Parallel heaps?
conceptual.answer: Hard—need concurrent priority structures (lock-free heaps advanced).

conceptual.question: External memory heap?
conceptual.answer: For data larger than RAM—layered with disk.

conceptual.question: Compare TreeSet vs PriorityQueue ordering?
conceptual.answer: TreeSet sorted unique; PQ allows duplicates, only min/max efficient.

codeBased.question: Top K frequent elements—heap approach.
codeBased.answer: Min-heap size k of pairs (freq,key) or bucket sort alternative.

codeBased.question: Merge k sorted lists with heap.
codeBased.answer: Poll min head, push next from that list.

codeBased.question: Implement heapify down for max heap.
codeBased.answer: Compare with left/right child swap down.

codeBased.question: PriorityQueue with Comparator.reverseOrder.
codeBased.answer: Max-heap for integers.

codeBased.question: kth largest element—quickselect vs heap?
codeBased.answer: Trade average time vs worst and memory.

codeBased.question: Sliding window median (hard)—data structures?
codeBased.answer: Two heaps or multiset—know existence.

codeBased.question: Dijkstra PQ relaxation sketch.
codeBased.answer: Dist map + PQ of nodes by tentative distance.

codeBased.question: Heap sort in-place swap root/end.
codeBased.answer: Classic loop.

codeBased.question: Insert into min heap array manually.
codeBased.answer: Add end, sift up.

codeBased.question: Why offer/poll vs add/remove in PriorityQueue?
codeBased.answer: add throws on failure; offer returns false—prefer offer in bounded settings.

seniorScenario.question: Job scheduler priority inversion?
seniorScenario.answer: Lower priority task holds lock needed by high—OS topic related to priorities.

seniorScenario.question: Storm of timer tasks in PQ—lag?
seniorScenario.answer: O(log n) each; millions of timers—time wheel alternative.

seniorScenario.question: GC root scanning vs heap?
seniorScenario.answer: Different “heap”—don’t confuse memory heap with data structure heap.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed priority queue?
seniorScenario.answer: Hard—often shard by priority bands or use external broker priority topics.

seniorScenario.question: Real-time system missed deadline—heap size?
seniorScenario.answer: Check priority ordering bugs and clock skew.

seniorScenario.question: Cache eviction LFU with heap?
seniorScenario.answer: Frequency counts + min-heap complexity—often combined structures.

wrongAnswers: Binary heap allows O(1) search for any element.
PriorityQueue in Java iterates elements in priority order.
Heaps are always implemented with pointers, never arrays.
Build heap from n elements is always Ω(n log n).
Max heap means largest element is at the leaves.
Heap sort is stable by default.

sections.title: Why Sorting and Binary Search matters
sections.content:
Sorting and binary search are foundational optimization tools; using them correctly often changes interview outcomes.

This day is central to DSA interviews because it combines coding speed with reasoning quality. Interviewers usually evaluate three things:
1. Can you identify the right pattern quickly?
2. Can you explain why the approach is correct?
3. Can you reason about complexity and edge cases?

Choosing sort strategy and writing robust binary search variants.

A high-quality answer should include:
- concise approach summary,
- small dry-run,
- complexity analysis,
- one alternative approach and trade-off.

By the end of Day 26, you should be able to solve representative problems and explain your decisions with confidence.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Sorting and Binary Search
sections.content:
### Why this topic matters now
Sorting and binary search are foundational optimization tools; using them correctly often changes interview outcomes.

### Core interview mental model
For Sorting and Binary Search, first identify the problem family, then map it to a known pattern. Pattern-first thinking reduces random trial-and-error.

### Implementation guidance
1. Write the simplest correct version first.
2. Validate using one normal and one edge case.
3. Improve complexity only after correctness is locked.
4. Refactor to readable helper methods.

### Complexity and trade-off lens
Do not state complexity by memory. Derive it from operations performed. Mention time + space and where each cost comes from.

### Common failure mode
Most failures in Sorting and Binary Search are not syntax errors. They come from broken invariants, weak boundary checks, or unclear state tracking.

### Interview speaking template
- "I considered X because..."
- "The invariant is..."
- "Per iteration we do..., so complexity is..."
- "An alternative would be..., but I chose this due to..."

### Production relevance
Even outside interviews, these patterns improve throughput, latency, and correctness in backend data processing tasks.

items: Mid calculation overflow in binary search on large bounds.
Using binary search on unsorted input.
Infinite loops from incorrect low/high update rules.
Not distinguishing lower_bound vs upper_bound style goals.
Overusing O(n log n) sort when linear approach exists.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: Stable sort definition?
conceptual.answer: Equal elements keep relative order—important for multi-key sorts.

conceptual.question: Which sorts are stable in Java?
conceptual.answer: TimSort (objects), merge sort stable; typical quicksort unstable.

conceptual.question: Quicksort average vs worst?
conceptual.answer: Average O(n log n); worst O(n²) bad pivot—randomize or median-of-three.

conceptual.question: Merge sort properties?
conceptual.answer: O(n log n) worst, O(n) extra space, stable.

conceptual.question: Insertion sort when good?
conceptual.answer: Small n nearly sorted—O(n) best case adaptive.

conceptual.question: Counting sort radix sort?
conceptual.answer: Linear when range constraints—not comparison lower bound.

conceptual.question: Binary search precondition?
conceptual.answer: Monotonic ordering on indexed access—array sorted or rotated variants with care.

conceptual.question: Find rotation point in rotated sorted array?
conceptual.answer: Modified binary search O(log n).

conceptual.question: Lower_bound / upper_bound concepts?
conceptual.answer: First position where predicate true—Arrays.binarySearch variants.

conceptual.question: External sort?
conceptual.answer: Merge sorted chunks from disk—streaming large data.

conceptual.question: Parallel sort Arrays.parallelSort?
conceptual.answer: ForkJoin for large primitive arrays—speed vs overhead.

conceptual.question: Inversion count?
conceptual.answer: Merge sort augmentation—count cross inversions while merging.

conceptual.question: Selection vs quickselect?
conceptual.answer: Quickselect finds kth element average O(n).

conceptual.question: Bitonic sort (mention)?
conceptual.answer: Parallel sorting networks—niche.

conceptual.question: Compare sort vs heap for “top K”.
conceptual.answer: Heap O(n log k) often beats full O(n log n) sort when k small.

codeBased.question: Binary search first >= target (lower bound).
codeBased.answer: lo/hi loop variants—know one by heart.

codeBased.question: Merge two sorted arrays in place (with extra space at end).
codeBased.answer: Three pointers from end.

codeBased.question: Quicksort partition Lomuto outline.
codeBased.answer: Pivot placement; i,j pointers.

codeBased.question: Count inversions merge step.
codeBased.answer: When a[i]>b[j] add remaining left elements to count.

codeBased.question: Arrays.sort int[] vs Integer[]?
codeBased.answer: Primitives dual-pivot quicksort; objects TimSort.

codeBased.question: Comparator nullsFirst for sort.
codeBased.answer: Handle null keys safely.

codeBased.question: Search insert position in sorted array.
codeBased.answer: Binary search boundary.

codeBased.question: Rotated array find minimum index.
codeBased.answer: Compare mid with right boundary.

codeBased.question: Stable counting sort for small range integers.
codeBased.answer: Prefix sums for positions.

codeBased.question: Parallel sort threshold?
codeBased.answer: JDK uses size threshold before parallel—tunable implementation detail.

seniorScenario.question: DB ORDER BY vs app sort—when which?
seniorScenario.answer: Large data: sort in DB with index; paginate—avoid loading all rows.

seniorScenario.question: Sort huge file on disk—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: External merge sort chunks; k-way merge.

seniorScenario.question: Non-transitive Comparator bug—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: IllegalArgumentException from TimSort or inconsistent ordering—deadly in production.

seniorScenario.question: Locale-sensitive sort mismatch?
seniorScenario.answer: Use Collator; binary sort wrong for human display.

seniorScenario.question: Distributed sort (MapReduce)?
seniorScenario.answer: Shuffle by key, sort partitions—different paradigm.

seniorScenario.question: Time-series data out-of-order ingest?
seniorScenario.answer: Sort windows or use data structures accepting disorder.

wrongAnswers: Arrays.sort on primitives uses TimSort.
Binary search works on unsorted array if you check each step.
Quicksort worst case cannot be avoided.
Merge sort is in-place with O(1) extra space typically.
Stable sort means equal elements swap randomly.
Binary search always returns an index when element missing.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Defining DP state ambiguously, causing wrong transitions.
Recursing without memoization and hitting exponential blow-up.
Using tabulation order that references uninitialized states.

Optimizing space before validating transition correctness.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is dynamic programming?
conceptual.answer: Break problem into overlapping subproblems; cache results to avoid exponential recomputation.

conceptual.question: Top-down vs bottom-up?
conceptual.answer: Memoization recursion vs iterative table filling—same math, different stack/space trade-offs.

conceptual.question: What is optimal substructure?
conceptual.answer: Optimal solution built from optimal solutions to subproblems.

conceptual.question: Classic 0/1 knapsack recurrence?
conceptual.answer: dp[i][w] max value using first i items with weight limit w—take or skip item.

conceptual.question: Unbounded knapsack difference?
conceptual.answer: Can reuse items—infinite loop variant in inner loop order.

conceptual.question: LCS longest common subsequence?
conceptual.answer: Match/mismatch recurrence; O(nm) table.

conceptual.question: Edit distance?
conceptual.answer: Insert/delete/substitute costs; DP table.

conceptual.question: Fibonacci memo vs tabulation?
conceptual.answer: Memo O(n) stack depth; tabulation O(1) space rolling.

conceptual.question: Coin change variants?
conceptual.answer: Count ways vs min coins—different recurrences; infinite vs finite coins.

conceptual.question: Palindrome DP?
conceptual.answer: Table[i][j] whether substring palindrome—expand or fill diagonally.

conceptual.question: State compression bitmask DP?
conceptual.answer: NP-hard subsets—2^n states—small n only.

conceptual.question: DAG shortest path as DP?
conceptual.answer: Topological order relaxation—linear time on DAG.

conceptual.question: When DP fails?
conceptual.answer: If optimal substructure or overlapping subproblems don’t hold—greedy may apply instead.

conceptual.question: Space optimization in DP?
conceptual.answer: If recurrence only needs previous row, keep two arrays or rolling variables.

conceptual.question: Digit DP (mention)?
conceptual.answer: Count numbers in range with digit constraints—competitive programming pattern.

codeBased.question: Fibonacci bottom-up O(n) time O(1) space.
codeBased.answer: Keep a,b iterate.

codeBased.question: Climbing stairs distinct ways—Fib variant.
codeBased.answer: dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2].

codeBased.question: House robber linear—adjacent constraint.
codeBased.answer: dp[i]=max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+nums[i]).

codeBased.question: Coin change min coins—BFS or DP.
codeBased.answer: Initialize INF; relax.

codeBased.question: LIS O(n log n) patience sorting.
codeBased.answer: Binary search on tails array.

codeBased.question: Matrix chain multiplication order?
codeBased.answer: Split at k; minimize cost—O(n³).

codeBased.question: Subset sum exists—boolean DP.
codeBased.answer: Bitset optimization possible.

codeBased.question: Decode ways string digits—DP.
codeBased.answer: Single/double digit transitions.

codeBased.question: Unique paths grid with obstacles.
codeBased.answer: dp[i][j] sum from top and left.

codeBased.question: Longest palindromic substring expand centers.
codeBased.answer: O(n²) time—also DP table possible.

seniorScenario.question: DP solution too slow in production—n large?
seniorScenario.answer: Greedy proof, approximation algorithms, or mathematical closed form.

seniorScenario.question: Memory blow-up 2D DP table?
seniorScenario.answer: Rolling array, sparse representation, or switch algorithm.

seniorScenario.question: Caching recursive microservice—DP analogy?
seniorScenario.answer: Memoization across requests—invalidate correctness carefully.

seniorScenario.question: Scheduling weighted interval DP?
seniorScenario.answer: Sort by end time; dp[i] max profit—classic.

seniorScenario.question: ML hyperparameter grid search vs DP?
seniorScenario.answer: Different—avoid confusing ML tuning with algorithmic DP.

seniorScenario.question: Stack overflow in deep memo recursion?
seniorScenario.answer: Convert to iterative DP.

wrongAnswers: DP always runs faster than brute force for every problem.
Greedy algorithms never work when DP applies.
Memoization and tabulation always use the same asymptotic time.
0/1 knapsack can be solved greedily by value/weight ratio always.
Fibonacci DP must keep entire table of size n.
Dynamic programming requires the problem to be NP-hard.
